narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, and I appreciate how challenging this process can feel. The admissions model we use predicted a 60% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above the threshold we set for approving applications—meaning your application was unfortunately rejected to minimize the risk of students struggling post-graduation. This prediction was driven primarily by your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, which is notably lower than the typical 3.21 average and stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting bar exam failure. While your Year 3 school decile ranking of 6 was actually better than average and helped pull the prediction in a more favorable direction toward passing, it wasn't quite enough to offset the GPA concern. Your middle family income bracket also contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction, as it aligns with patterns where additional resources can make a difference in bar prep success. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 36, close to the 36.7 average, and your Year 1 decile of 5 provided modest support toward passing, as did your full-time enrollment status, which is the norm for 93% of students. Compared to typical applicants, your academic profile shows some strengths like that solid later decile and LSAT, but the lower undergrad GPA was the standout issue relative to peers who generally pass the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just the model's data-driven read on bar passage odds. To move forward, I recommend retaking undergrad-level courses or a GPA-boosting program to strengthen that key area, while leveraging your LSAT and full-time commitment. Consider targeted bar prep resources tailored for students from middle-income backgrounds like yours. Reapplying after addressing the GPA could shift the prediction in your favor—many students in similar situations succeed with focused improvements. We're rooting for you to turn this around.
